title: API Security in 2026 — The Complete Guide for Backend Developers
excerpt: API security breaches cost companies millions. Most vulnerabilities are not sophisticated attacks — they are basic mistakes that any developer can prevent. This is the checklist every backend developer must follow.
coverImageAlt: API Security Best Practices 2026 — ArivuOn Academy

seo.metaTitle: API Security Best Practices 2026 — Complete Guide for Developers | ArivuOn Academy
seo.metaDescription: Learn API security in 2026. Authentication, authorization, rate limiting, SQL injection prevention, JWT best practices — complete backend security guide by ArivuOn Academy.

content.text: In 2025, API attacks accounted for 83% of all web application breaches according to industry reports. The most common vulnerabilities were not zero-day exploits — they were authentication flaws, missing authorization checks, and exposed sensitive data. Every one of them preventable. This guide is the security checklist every backend developer must internalize.
The OWASP API Security Top 10 — What Actually Gets Exploited

steps.title: Broken Object Level Authorization (BOLA)
skills: Check ownership on every object access
Never trust client-provided IDs alone
Example: /api/orders/456 — verify 456 belongs to the requesting user
Django: always filter by request.user
steps.why: The #1 API vulnerability for 5 years running. An attacker changes order_id=123 to order_id=124 and reads another user's order. Takes one line of code to prevent, costs millions when exploited.

steps.title: Broken Authentication
skills: JWT: validate signature AND expiry on every request
Refresh token rotation — invalidate old tokens on use
Rate limit login endpoints
Never store passwords in plain text (use bcrypt/argon2)
steps.why: Weak authentication is the front door being unlocked. Once bypassed, every other security measure is irrelevant.

steps.title: Excessive Data Exposure
skills: Return only fields the client actually needs
Never serialize full ORM objects to API responses
Use explicit serializer fields (whitelist, not blacklist)
Audit every API response for sensitive fields
steps.why: APIs that return full database rows often leak passwords (hashed but still), internal IDs, personal data. Define explicit response schemas.

steps.title: Lack of Rate Limiting
skills: Rate limit all authentication endpoints (max 5 attempts/min)
Rate limit expensive computation endpoints
Use sliding window rate limiting (Redis)
Return 429 Too Many Requests with Retry-After header
steps.why: Without rate limiting, password brute-force attacks, credential stuffing, and API abuse are trivial. One compromised endpoint can bring down your entire service.

steps.title: Security Misconfiguration
skills: HTTPS everywhere — no HTTP in production
CORS configured restrictively (not *)
Security headers: HSTS, X-Frame-Options, CSP
Debug mode OFF in production
steps.why: Misconfiguration is the most common cause of breaches. A Django app with DEBUG=True in production exposes your entire settings file including database credentials.

content.title: Secure Django API Pattern — BOLA Prevention + Rate Limiting
Never Do This With JWTs
content.text: Never store sensitive data in JWT payload — it is base64 encoded, not encrypted. Anyone can decode it. Never use 'none' algorithm. Always validate the algorithm in the header matches what your server expects. Set short expiry (15-60 minutes) and use refresh tokens for long-lived sessions.

items.question: JWT vs Session-based authentication — which should I use?
items.answer: Session-based (server stores session state) for traditional web apps — simpler, more secure by default, easy to invalidate. JWT (stateless — client holds the token) for APIs consumed by mobile apps, SPAs, or third-party clients. JWT's weakness: you cannot truly invalidate a token before expiry without a server-side blocklist, which defeats the 'stateless' benefit. For most Django APIs, JWT with short expiry + refresh token rotation is the right choice.

items.question: How do I protect against SQL injection in Django?
items.answer: The ORM protects you by default — always use it. The vulnerability appears when you write raw SQL with string interpolation: cursor.execute('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ' + user_id) — never do this. If you must write raw SQL, use parameterized queries: cursor.execute('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = %s', [user_id]). The %s placeholder escapes the input safely.

items.question: What security headers should every API have?
items.answer: Critical: Strict-Transport-Security (forces HTTPS), X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff (prevents MIME sniffing), X-Frame-Options: DENY (prevents clickjacking). For APIs: remove X-Powered-By and Server headers (do not advertise your stack). For SPAs: Content-Security-Policy. In Django, use django-security or manually add these in your middleware. Check your headers at securityheaders.com.
